output:
# MONOSHOT and QUORUM: From a Single Global Shot-Count to a Per-Query, Topology-Conditional, Partially-Pooled Neighbor Budget for k-NN-style In-Context Learning, with Provable Exact Collapse to Fixed-Shot ICL

A research proposal generated from the Introduction of "In-Context Learning or: How I Learned
to Stop Worrying and Love Applied Information Retrieval" (Parry, Ganguly, and Chandra, SIGIR
2024; arXiv:2405.01116), which frames in-context learning (ICL) as a non-parametric, k-NN-style
predictor whose answer depends on the local topology of a retrieved set of labelled examples.

This document follows a reusable diagnostic lens that has been instantiated across many machine
learning domains: locate a place where a method applies a SINGLE GLOBAL SCALAR identically to
every instance, blind to an observable covariate that controls the instance-specific optimum;
show that the harm of this representational choice is SILENT under the field's pooled metric,
SCALES with heterogeneity, and VANISHES under homogeneity; then replace the scalar with a
structured, monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map of the observable covariate that
PROVABLY COLLAPSES to the original method at its default setting. Here the scalar is the number
of in-context demonstrations (shots) k; the covariate is the query's observable local topology.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 In-context learning, the verbaliser, and the k-NN analogy

Large language models (LLMs) trained either as masked language models (BERT and successors) or
as causal/auto-regressive language models (the GPT, Llama and Mistral families [2, 36, 37])
acquire, at scale and after instruction tuning, the ability to adapt to a new task from a few
labelled examples placed in the prompt -- in-context learning (ICL) [2]. For a predictive task
such as text classification, each class is named by a set of words (a verbaliser [18]), and the
decoder's posterior over the vocabulary is mapped to the most likely class [2, 16, 18]. A large
and fast-moving literature (surveyed in [8]) studies how to make ICL work: which demonstrations to
choose [3, 4], how to order them [5], how to calibrate the decoder's bias [7, 11], how many labels
it can juggle [16], and why it works at all [6, 12].

The source paper [1] crystallises an analogy that the present proposal takes as its seed: ICL is
"conceptually similar to a non-parametric approach, such as k-NN, where the prediction for each
instance essentially depends on the local topology, i.e., on a localised set of similar
instances and their labels (called few-shot examples)". The only structural difference from
k-NN, [1] argues, is that ICL routes the localised evidence through a frozen LLM rather than a
majority vote. This reframes ICL as applied information retrieval (IR): a test instance is a
query, the labelled training pool is a collection, and the few-shot examples are the top-ranked
retrieved documents. The classical k-NN reference points are the nearest-neighbour rule itself
[29] and its locally adaptive descendants [30].

### 1.2 Retrieval-based, k-NN-style ICL and demonstration selection

Operationalising the analogy, retrieval-based ICL retrieves, for each test query x, the
top-scoring demonstrations from a labelled pool under a frozen similarity function (sparse such
as BM25, or dense such as Sentence-BERT [24] or Contriever-style encoders), and appends them to
the prompt. KATE [3] showed that nearest-neighbour demonstration selection beats random
selection; EPR [4] learns a retriever whose objective is to rank "useful" demonstrations first;
LM-BFF [17] couples retrieval with automatic verbalisers; diversity-aware selection [13] and
annotation-budget-aware selection (AdaICL [14]) refine which examples enter the prompt. A
parallel line makes the k-NN step explicit at inference: k-NN-LM [23] interpolates a parametric
LM with a nearest-neighbour distribution over a datastore, and k-NN-Prompting [9] performs
nearest-neighbour inference over the LLM's own representations to scale beyond the context
window. Across essentially all of this work, one design choice is held fixed and global: the
NUMBER of demonstrations k (the number of neighbours), tuned once on a development set and then
applied identically to every test query.

### 1.3 The shot-count axis: static k, and the source paper's AICL proposal

How many shots to use is known to matter and to behave erratically: accuracy is sensitive to the
demonstration set and its order [5, 7], more shots are not monotonically better and can inject
majority-label and recency bias [6, 7, 11], yet very long contexts (many-shot ICL [15]) can help
on some tasks -- so the optimum is task- and instance-dependent, not a universal constant. The
source paper [1] is, to our knowledge, the first to name the per-query version of this axis: its
first proposed IR-to-ICL mapping, "Adaptive ICL" (AICL), argues that "instead of using a
constant number of examples for each instance ... make the number of examples variable", and
ties this to two classical IR problems -- predicting a rank cutoff (how many documents to read
down a ranked list [26]) and query performance prediction (QPP [25]), the task of estimating,
without relevance labels, how hard a query is and how good its retrieved results are. The stated
AICL heuristic is to "use a higher number of examples if the example quality is predicted to be
poor", and the paper's preliminary evaluation reports that "adjusting the number of examples in
a data-driven manner does lead to significant improvements". AICL is presented as a perspective
plus a preliminary study, not a fully specified, validated estimator.

### 1.4 What stays global, and what is missing

The source paper therefore opens exactly the right door -- the shot count should be a per-query
quantity driven by an observable signal of local example quality -- but leaves two questions
that determine whether such a method is reliable and deployable:

(i) HOW should the observable signal be turned into a count? A per-query QPP signal is notoriously
noisy [25]; a free per-query count predictor will overfit that noise precisely in the data-poor
tail (sparse regions, rare classes) where the count matters most, while a single global cutoff
under-fits the heterogeneity it is meant to capture. The classical k-NN literature already
learned this lesson -- locally adaptive neighbourhoods help but must be regularised [30] -- and
the statistics of estimating many related quantities from little data each was settled by
empirical-Bayes / partial pooling (James-Stein and multilevel models [31, 32]).

(ii) WHAT happens at the default? An adaptive-count method that can silently underperform the
plain static-k baseline it replaces is hard to adopt. None of the above lines provides a count
rule that is GUARANTEED, by construction, to reduce to standard fixed-shot ICL.

These are not incidental engineering gaps; they are the difference between "variable k helps on
average in a preliminary study" and "a structured, tail-validated, do-no-harm adaptive-count
ICL". They are also invisible to the metric the field reports (pooled accuracy), which is the
binding obstacle analysed in Section 2.

### 1.5 The reusable diagnostic lens and glossary

We import a lens that has repeatedly converted "a tuned global constant" into "a covariate-
conditional partially-pooled map with exact collapse" across federated learning, graph neural
networks, multimodal tokenisation, preference alignment, RLHF safety gating, quantum kernels,
NeRF style transfer, and benchmark diagnostics. Two ingredients recur and are load-bearing here:
hierarchical PARTIAL POOLING / empirical Bayes [31, 32] to estimate many per-stratum quantities
without overfitting, and an EXACT-COLLAPSE identity that makes the new method a strict
generalisation of the old one, gateable in continuous integration. Subpopulation-aware and
behavioural evaluation [27, 28] supplies the stratified measurement that makes an otherwise
metric-silent gap visible.

Glossary (all notation is plain ASCII):
- k: number of in-context demonstrations (shots / retrieved neighbours) for a query.
- k_global: the single global static shot count of the host method (its tuned default).
- x: a test query (instance to be classified). P: the labelled demonstration pool.
- d_x: an observable, leakage-free per-query local-topology covariate (a QPP-style estimate of
  neighbourhood reliability / example quality), computed from the frozen retriever's scores
  between x and P and from the pool labels (never the test label).
- d_0: an anchor value of d (e.g. the development-set median), at which the map is the identity.
- g_theta: an anchored monotone map with g_theta(0) = 0 and no surviving constant term, so that
  theta = 0 makes g_theta identically zero for ALL inputs.
- k(d_x) = clamp_[k_min, k_max]( round( k_global + g_theta(d_x - d_0) ) ): the per-query budget.
- p_base(y | x): host fixed-shot ICL predictive distribution; p_QUORUM(y | x): ours.
- E_collapse = max_x || p_QUORUM(. | x) - p_base(. | x) ||_inf: the exact-collapse gate quantity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

### 2.1 The gap: MONOSHOT (a Monolithic, query-invariant shot-count)

We name the representational gap MONOSHOT: standard retrieval-based, k-NN-style ICL appends a
SINGLE GLOBAL number of demonstrations k_global to EVERY query, blind to the query's observable
local topology. A scalar k_global cannot equal the non-constant per-query optimum k*(x):

- For a query that sits in a DENSE, label-PURE neighbourhood (many close, same-label examples),
  a large k drags in farther, lower-similarity, possibly off-label demonstrations that dilute the
  prompt, amplify majority-label and recency bias [6, 7, 11], and can flip an otherwise-correct
  prediction. Such queries are OVER-SHOT.
- For a query in a SPARSE, label-MIXED neighbourhood (few close examples, conflicting labels),
  a small k supplies too little local evidence to specify the decision. Such queries are
  UNDER-SHOT.

The per-query optimum k*(x) is thus a non-constant function of local topology; the exact shape
(more shots when neighbourhood quality is poor, as [1] conjectures, versus the opposite) is an
empirical question we treat as a TESTED inductive bias rather than assume. The representational
claim needs only that k*(x) is non-constant across the query population.

This gap has the three diagnostic signatures of the lens:

- SILENT under pooled accuracy. Over-shooting harms dense-region queries and under-shooting
  harms sparse-region queries; these are opposite-signed errors over disjoint, often minority
  sub-populations, so they partially cancel in the pooled-accuracy mean. The single best global
  k therefore looks like a flat, healthy optimum while both topology tails are mis-served. (This
  is why even AICL's [1] "significant" pooled improvement cannot, by itself, reveal whether the
  data-poor tail was helped or hurt.)
- SCALES with topology heterogeneity. The gap grows with the spread (variance / bimodality) of
  local density and label purity across the query population -- largest for fine-grained, high-
  label-cardinality, or domain-shifted classification (e.g. Banking77 [34], CLINC150 [35], many-
  label settings [16]), where neighbourhoods differ wildly in reliability.
- VANISHES under homogeneity. If every query has the same local topology, k*(x) = k_global for
  all x and the global scalar is already optimal; there is nothing to gain. A correct method must
  therefore show NO benefit on a topology-homogeneous population (falsifier T1, Section 3).

### 2.2 The binding obstacle: metric blindness

The obstacle that makes MONOSHOT both real and previously unaddressed is that the field's
instrument -- pooled top-1 accuracy at the single best global k -- is largely blind to it. Because
the over-/under-shooting errors offset in the mean, the pooled optimum is a compromise that is
locally suboptimal across much of the query space yet globally flat. Worse, the one prior attempt
to vary the count (AICL [1]) is itself validated on pooled accuracy, so a per-query count predictor
that overfits the noisy QPP signal in the sparse tail -- the regime where adaptation is most
tempting and most dangerous -- can post a healthy pooled number while degrading exactly the queries
that already
get the worst service. Detecting the gap requires topology-stratified evaluation, semi-synthetic
data with a known per-query optimal k, and the falsifier ladder of Section 3. Without this
instrumentation the gap is undetectable, which is precisely why a single tuned k, or an
unstructured adaptive rule, has survived.

### 2.3 The method: QUORUM (a Query-conditioned, partially-pooled neighbour-budget map)

QUORUM replaces the scalar k_global with a per-query budget

  k(d_x) = clamp_[k_min, k_max]( round( k_global + g_theta(d_x - d_0) ) ),

assembled from four pieces, each chosen to defeat one obstacle:

1. Observable, leakage-free covariate d_x (defeats over-fitting to the wrong signal and label
   leakage). d_x is a QPP-style estimate of the query's local-topology reliability, computed
   ONLY from the frozen retriever's similarity scores between x and the pool P and from the pool
   labels: a multi-axis, rank-normalised fusion of (a) local density / similarity decay (the
   absolute similarity of the top neighbours and how fast it falls with rank), (b) local label
   purity / entropy among the top-M (M >= k_max, fixed) candidate neighbours, and (c) score-margin
   features at FIXED reference ranks known before prediction (e.g. the best-vs-k_global and
   best-vs-M similarity gaps within the top-M list). None of these uses the test label or the
   to-be-computed budget k(d_x), so d_x is leakage-free and well-posed with respect to the
   prediction target. This is the very QPP signal the source paper [1] proposes for AICL, reused
   here as a covariate rather than consumed by an unstructured cutoff.

2. Anchored monotone map g_theta (defeats both the global-constant under-fit and sign ambiguity).
   g_theta(u) = sum_k theta_k ( phi_k(u) - phi_k(0) ) with phi_k monotone non-decreasing basis
   functions and free coefficients; the subtraction of phi_k(0) leaves NO constant term, so
   theta = 0 makes g_theta identically zero for every u. The monotone DIRECTION (the source's
   intuition is: poorer neighbourhood quality -> larger k) is a learned, tested inductive bias,
   with a reversed / non-monotone variant as ablation A6.

3. Hierarchical partial pooling / empirical Bayes (defeats the data-poverty obstacle). The
   PRIMARY QUORUM estimator is hierarchically partially pooled across K ordered topology strata:
   each stratum receives an offset delta_k ~ Normal(0, tau^2) to the shared monotone backbone map,
   tau^2 fit by marginal maximum likelihood, so data-poor strata shrink toward the shared global
   map and only data-rich strata move. The shrunken per-stratum levels are projected onto a
   monotone (isotonic) sequence in stratum order, so the combined map remains non-decreasing in
   d_x. This estimator spans a pooling continuum whose two endpoints are explicit ablations (A4):
   tau^2 -> 0 is the FULLY-POOLED limit (a single shared monotone map, one theta), and tau^2 ->
   infinity is the UNPOOLED-STRATUM limit (each stratum's offset fit independently, no shrinkage).
   The still-finer unpooled FREE PER-QUERY predictor is a distinct, even-noisier rule -- the
   AICL-style baseline B5c -- not an endpoint of this stratum hierarchy. QUORUM is the
   partially-pooled interior -- the bias-variance-correct realisation of AICL's idea. Note that the
   do-no-harm DEFAULT of Section 2.4 is the separate choice theta = 0 (exact collapse), independent
   of the pooling level; partial pooling governs how the ACTIVE map is estimated, not whether the
   safety identity holds.

4. Layer and cost (defeats the deployability obstacle). QUORUM acts ONLY at the retrieval /
   prompt-assembly layer: it reuses the host's frozen retriever and frozen LLM unchanged and adds
   no trainable model and no extra LLM forward pass (d_x is read off retrieval-side scores the
   pipeline already produces; a modest top-M retrieval with M >= k_max replaces top-k_global on
   the retrieval side). The pipeline -- normalisation, anchor d_0, strata, pooling, theta, clamps
   -- is fit on a held-in development split via cross-fitting and frozen before any test query is
   scored. Because dense/easy queries can use FEWER shots, the average prompt length (and thus
   LLM latency and cost) can DROP relative to fixed-k, a deployability bonus we measure rather
   than assume.

An OPTIONAL second knob for hosts that make the k-NN vote explicit (e.g. k-NN-Prompting [9]) is a
per-query temperature on the distance-to-weight kernel; it is orthogonal, scoped as future work,
and not required for the primary result.

### 2.4 The exact-collapse identity

QUORUM is a strict generalisation of fixed-shot ICL. When theta = 0 (so g_theta is identically
zero) and the clamp is inactive at the default (k_min <= k_global <= k_max), then for EVERY query

  k(d_x) = clamp( round( k_global + 0 ) ) = k_global,

because k_global is an integer and rounding a constant introduces no boundary ambiguity. QUORUM
is required, by construction, to use the host's EXACT retrieval backend and ranking: the top-M
candidate list (M >= k_max) is the host ranking truncated at M with the SAME deterministic
tie-breaking, so for any k <= M the QUORUM top-k equals the host's top-k as an ordered set,
ties included. Hence an identical budget k(d_x) = k_global yields the IDENTICAL ordered
demonstration set, a byte-identical prompt, and -- under the same frozen LLM, with the predictive
distribution read from the deterministic decoder posterior over the verbaliser -- an identical
prediction: p_QUORUM(. | x) = p_base(. | x) for all x. The exact-collapse gate is

  E_collapse = max_x || p_QUORUM(. | x) - p_base(. | x) ||_inf <= 1e-6,

which in exact arithmetic equals zero; the 1e-6 tolerance only absorbs non-deterministic GPU
floating-point reordering. As a hard implementation requirement (and a belt-and-suspenders CI
check) the theta = 0 configuration MUST dispatch to the host retrieval/prompt code path exactly,
so the identity is enforced, not merely expected. Setting k_global to the host's tuned best
fixed shot count recovers that host EXACTLY. Two cautions inherited from prior instantiations of
the lens: (i) a CONSTANT covariate with theta != 0 yields a SHIFTED global count, not the host,
so the homogeneous-null falsifier T1 must compare against a RE-TUNED best-global-k baseline (B4),
which is distinct from this theta = 0 identity guard that holds on ANY data; (ii) the rounding is
part of the active method, not the collapse point, so it never threatens the identity.

### 2.5 The novelty wedge C1-C4 and why prior lines stop at two

QUORUM occupies the joint of four clauses:
- C1: acts only at the retrieval / prompt-assembly (shot-count) layer; reuses the frozen
  retriever and LLM; no new model, no extra inference.
- C2: conditions on an observable, leakage-free per-query local-topology covariate d_x.
- C3: a structured, monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map (not a single global scalar,
  not an unstructured cutoff, not a free per-query predictor).
- C4: a provable exact collapse to the fixed-shot host, E_collapse <= 1e-6.

No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4 (the six near-miss families are enumerated in
Question 4). The sharpest, the source paper's own AICL [1], covers C1 and C2 -- it varies the
count per query from an observable QPP signal -- but misses C3 (it maps the signal to a count via
a data-driven cutoff / binning or a free predictor, not a partially-pooled monotone map) and C4
(it offers no enforced exact-collapse identity and can underperform static-k). QUORUM is thus the
structured, shrinkage-regularised, do-no-harm realisation of the very direction [1] proposes:
same door, but the estimator and the safety guarantee that make it reliable in the tail and safe
to deploy.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Section 3. Novel Research Idea (the five core questions)

**Here is a high-level summarized insight of a research field Machine Learning.** Retrieval-based,
k-NN-style in-context learning chooses a single global number of demonstrations for every query;
because the harm of this choice cancels under pooled accuracy, the field has tuned that number as
a constant, and the one proposal to vary it per query (AICL) lacks both a regularised estimator
and a do-no-harm guarantee; promoting the shot count to a monotone, partially-pooled map of an
observable local-topology covariate, with provable exact collapse to fixed-shot ICL, fixes a
representational gap that the pooled metric hides.

**Here are the five core questions:**

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can the single global number of in-context demonstrations that retrieval-based, k-NN-style
in-context learning appends to every test query be replaced by a per-query, topology-conditional
shot count -- a structured, monotone, partially-pooled map of an observable, leakage-free
local-topology covariate computed from the frozen retriever's own scores -- that improves
text-classification accuracy on topology-heterogeneous query populations while provably
collapsing to the original fixed-shot in-context learner at its default setting?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The shot count is the most basic dial in in-context learning, set in essentially every ICL system
and every retrieval-augmented prompt, yet it is almost universally a single tuned constant. Five
implications follow from getting it right.

First, it operationalises, rigorously, the central analogy of the seed paper [1]: if ICL is
k-NN over a retrieved neighbourhood, then a fixed shot count is a fixed neighbourhood size, and
decades of work on locally adaptive neighbourhoods [30] and on predicting a rank cutoff / query
difficulty [25, 26] say the neighbourhood size should track the local topology. QUORUM is the
bridge that carries that IR/k-NN knowledge into the ICL prompt-assembly layer with a correctness
guarantee, exactly the cross-pollination [1] calls for.

Second, it converts a metric-silent failure into a measured, fixable one. By construction the gap
hides under pooled accuracy; a topology-stratified, semi-synthetic, falsifier-gated protocol
makes it visible and gives the community an instrument to audit any adaptive-count method
(including [1]) for tail behaviour, not just average lift.

Third, it makes adaptive-count ICL SAFE to adopt. The exact-collapse identity gives the method an
exact disable/default path that recovers the static-k baseline it generalises (a do-no-harm
DEFAULT, shadow-deployable), so turning the method on can never be worse than the baseline at that
default; no-regression for LEARNED active settings is a separate, empirically enforced
pre-registered requirement, not a consequence of the collapse. This default-recovery guarantee is
the property practitioners need, and it is the missing piece that separates a deployable system
from a preliminary study.

Fourth, it is efficiency-relevant. Because dense/easy queries can be served with fewer shots, an
adaptive budget can match or beat fixed-k accuracy at LOWER average prompt length, i.e. lower
token cost and latency -- a potential Pareto improvement on the accuracy-versus-budget frontier
that matters at deployment scale and for long-context many-shot regimes [15].

Fifth, the recipe is host-agnostic and template-general: the same scalar-to-map promotion applies
to any retrieval-augmented predictor with a tunable retrieval depth, so a positive result seeds a
family of "promote the global cutoff to a partially-pooled covariate map with exact collapse"
methods across retrieval-augmented NLP.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

Six obstacles stand between the idea and a credible result; the last is binding.

O1 (entangled per-query optimum). k*(x) is confounded with query difficulty, class frequency,
prompt length, retriever quality, and verbaliser calibration [7, 11]; a naive correlation of k
with raw accuracy mixes these. Mitigation: residualise the covariate on difficulty/length and
evaluate on semi-synthetic data with a KNOWN per-query optimal k.

O2 (data poverty in the tail). The sparse, rare-class queries where the count matters most are
exactly where per-query estimates are noisiest; a free per-query predictor overfits there. This
is what makes naive AICL [1] risky and is the reason partial pooling [31, 32] is mandatory, not
optional, in the estimator.

O3 (observability and leakage-freeness). The covariate must be computable at inference from
frozen-retriever scores and pool labels only, never the test label, and the whole pipeline must
be fit in-fold and frozen before test scoring; a covariate that peeks at the answer would inflate
results and break deployability.

O4 (multi-axis topology). Local density, label purity, and score margin are distinct axes that
disagree (a dense but label-mixed neighbourhood is not the same as a sparse pure one); a single
proxy is brittle, so the covariate must fuse axes, rank-normalised, and be ablated (A1).

O5 (joint lift, no-regression, and exact collapse). The method must simultaneously help the tail,
not regress the pooled metric, and reduce exactly to static-k at the default -- the anchored,
no-constant-term map plus integer-rounding-of-a-constant is what makes the third compatible with
the first two; a generic per-query regressor cannot guarantee the collapse.

O6 (BINDING -- metric blindness). Pooled top-1 accuracy at the best global k averages the
opposite-signed over-/under-shooting errors into a flat, healthy-looking optimum, and it also
masks whether an adaptive rule helps or hurts the data-poor tail. Naive approaches fail not
because adaptive k is wrong but because the instrument used to judge it cannot see the quantity
being optimised; without topology-stratified, semi-synthetic, and covariate-scramble evaluation
the gap is invisible and any adaptive-count claim (including a pooled "significant improvement")
is uninterpretable at the tail. MAKE IT CLEAR: the difficulty is as much measurement as method.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior work clusters into six families; each covers at most two of C1-C4, and the barrier explains
why.

NM1 (SHARPEST -- the source's AICL [1]). Varies the shot count per query from a QPP/rank-cutoff
signal: covers C1 (prompt-assembly layer) and C2 (observable signal). It misses C3 -- it maps the
signal to a count via a data-driven cutoff/binning or, in spirit, a free per-query predictor, not
a monotone hierarchically partially-pooled map -- and C4, offering no enforced exact-collapse
identity and able to underperform static-k. To our reading it is a preliminary perspective, not a
regularised, tail-validated estimator. QUORUM is its structured, do-no-harm completion.

NM2 (fixed-k demonstration selection). KATE [3], EPR [4], LM-BFF [17], diverse selection [13],
annotation-aware selection (AdaICL) [14] choose WHICH demonstrations and in what ORDER [5] at a
FIXED global k: cover C1, miss C2 (no per-query count covariate), C3, and C4. They are the static
collapse target for the count, orthogonal to it.

NM3 (adaptive-retrieval RAG). Adaptive-retrieval extensions of retrieval-augmented generation
[19] -- Adaptive-RAG [20], Self-RAG [21], and active retrieval / FLARE [22] -- decide WHETHER and
how much to retrieve per query via a trained router or model-confidence signal for open-domain
generation: they change the retrieval STRATEGY/pipeline
(missing C1's within-fixed-pipeline, prompt-assembly-only constraint), use a router classifier or
self-critique rather than a monotone partially-pooled map (miss C3), and provide no exact collapse
to a fixed-shot classifier (miss C4); their signal is a model-predicted complexity label, not the
observable retrieval-topology covariate (brushing C2).

NM4 (explicit-k-NN inference). k-NN-LM [23] and k-NN-Prompting [9] perform nearest-neighbour
inference with a FIXED global k and global interpolation/temperature: cover C1 and the k-NN
spirit, miss C2 (no per-query count covariate), C3, and C4 (no collapse to fixed-shot ICL).

NM5 (per-query selection/ordering). Self-Adaptive ICL [10] selects and orders demonstrations per
query by an information-compression criterion: per-query adaptation (spirit of C2) at the
prompt-assembly layer (C1), but it adapts the SET and ORDER at a fixed COUNT, with no
partially-pooled count map (miss C3) and no exact-collapse identity (miss C4).

NM6 (classical adaptive-k / variable-bandwidth k-NN). Discriminant Adaptive Nearest Neighbour
[30] and variable-k rules adapt the neighbourhood locally in CLASSICAL feature space: they
predate the LLM prompt-assembly layer (miss C1), offer no exact collapse to an ICL host (miss
C4), and are typically unpooled heuristics (miss C3).

Barrier. The shot count was treated as a single global hyperparameter to tune on pooled accuracy;
where the field did propose adapting it [1], it used an unstructured QPP cutoff or a free per-query
predictor and validated on pooled accuracy, leaving the data-poor tail hidden and offering no
do-no-harm guarantee; the bias-variance-correct estimator (monotone map plus hierarchical partial
pooling) and the exact-collapse safety identity were never co-targeted, and the k-NN literature's
variable-k machinery stayed classical and never crossed into the ICL prompt-assembly layer with a
collapse guarantee. MAKE IT CLEAR: QUORUM differs by being, simultaneously, (a) covariate-
conditional on observable local topology, (b) a structured monotone PARTIALLY-POOLED map rather
than a global cutoff or a free predictor, and (c) provably exact-collapsing to fixed-shot ICL --
the joint that no prior line attains.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method. Host H1 = standard retrieval-based ICL (dense retriever Sentence-BERT [24] or sparse
BM25 over the labelled pool, top-k_global demonstrations, verbaliser decoding [16, 18]); the
PRIMARY exact-collapse target. The sharpest competitors are a faithful FAMILY of AICL-style
adaptive-count rules [1] (QPP-driven count selection; see baseline B5), tuned under a common
budget so the comparison is not against a single weak instantiation. For a query x: (1) retrieve
the top-M candidates (M >= k_max fixed) and read off the covariate d_x = rank-normalised fusion of
local similarity decay, top-M label purity/entropy, and fixed-reference-rank score margins
(best-vs-k_global and best-vs-M); (2) compute
k(d_x) = clamp_[k_min,k_max]( round( k_global + g_theta(d_x - d_0) ) ) with the anchored monotone
basis map; (3) prompt the frozen LLM with the top-k(d_x) demonstrations in the host's order and
decode. The pipeline (normalisation, anchor d_0, K strata, empirical-Bayes tau^2, theta, clamps)
is fit by cross-fitting on a held-in development split of the labelled pool and frozen before test
scoring. The PRIMARY estimator is the EB partial-pooling map over K = 5 topology strata; the
fully-pooled single shared map and the unpooled-stratum estimator are its limiting ablations (A4),
while the finer unpooled per-query rule is the separate AICL-style baseline B5c.

Datasets. Real text classification spanning low to high label cardinality and topology
heterogeneity: SST-2 and sentiment (Yelp/Amazon polarity from [33]), topic (AG News, DBpedia
from [33]), question type (TREC), NLI (RTE/CB/MNLI via GLUE [38]), and high-cardinality intent
(Banking77 [34], CLINC150 [35]) and many-label settings [16] where neighbourhood reliability
varies most. SEMI-SYNTHETIC controlled-topology suites construct query populations with a KNOWN
per-query optimal k by controlling neighbourhood density and label purity in embedding space (and
a tunable heterogeneity spread for the dose axis, plus a homogeneous-topology null). LLMs (frozen,
reused, no training): GPT-2-XL, GPT-J-6B, Llama-2-7B/13B [36], and Mistral-7B [37], giving a
model-size and family sweep.

Baselines. B1 zero-shot (k = 0); B2 random demonstrations at k_global; B3 retrieval fixed
best-global-k (KATE/EPR-style [3, 4]; PRIMARY collapse target and theta = 0 identity guard); B4
retrieval RE-TUNED best-global-k on the homogeneous-null subset (for falsifier T1, distinct from
the identity guard); B5 a FAMILY of faithful AICL-style adaptive-count baselines [1] under a
common tuning budget -- B5a thresholded-QPP cutoff, B5b binned QPP-to-k map, B5c unpooled monotone
QPP-to-k map (the sharpest, unstructured competitors); B6 covariate-scrambled QUORUM (placebo);
B7 per-query oracle k (semi-synthetic upper bound); B8 QUORUM fully-pooled single shared map
(ablation); B9 QUORUM with EB partial pooling (the PRIMARY reported method).

Metrics. PRIMARY: topology-tail-stratified accuracy (worst local-reliability decile -- the
sparse/label-mixed queries) plus, on semi-synthetic data, per-query optimal-k recovery error and
the monotone-dose response, all reported at MATCHED-OR-LOWER average shot budget. SECONDARY:
pooled accuracy (and macro-F1 for high-cardinality sets, so rare classes are not averaged away)
no-regression versus B3 at a pre-registered margin (bootstrap 95% CI or 0.5 point, whichever
larger); topology-stratified accuracy slope; covariate (QPP) calibration AUROC; average shot
budget / token cost / latency; and the guardrail E_collapse <= 1e-6.

Ablations. A1 covariate choice (density vs purity vs score-margin vs fused); A2 monotone basis
family; A3 number of strata K; A4 pooling strength tau^2 (fully pooled to unpooled-stratum); A5 clamp band
[k_min, k_max]; A6 map DIRECTION (learned vs reversed vs non-monotone); A7 retriever (dense vs
BM25) and LLM size/family.

Falsifiers. T1 homogeneous-null: on a topology-homogeneous population QUORUM must NOT beat the
RE-TUNED best-global-k B4 (distinct from the theta = 0 identity guard, which holds on any data).
T2 monotone-dose: the tail gain must GROW with injected topology heterogeneity and vanish at zero
spread. T3 covariate-scramble: permuting d_x across queries (B6) must destroy the gain. Any
failure refutes the mechanism rather than a hyperparameter.

Hypotheses (numeric ranges are conjectured, with no preliminary results). H1: QUORUM lifts
worst-decile topology-tail accuracy by roughly 2-8 points over B3 while keeping the pooled metric
within the pre-registered no-regression margin. H2 (Simpson-like): the pooled number hides a
help/hurt split across topology strata, and the naive unstructured AICL-style baselines (B5) HURT
the sparse tail they most try to help. H3: EB partial pooling cuts per-stratum k-estimate variance
by roughly 30-60% versus the unpooled limit (A4), concentrated in the data-poor tail. H4:
covariate-scramble (B6) erases at
least 80% of the gain. H5: a monotone dose-response -- gain grows with heterogeneity and is null
on the homogeneous set. H6: E_collapse <= 1e-6 and exact static-k recovery hold by construction,
and QUORUM Pareto-improves the accuracy-versus-average-shot-budget frontier (matching B3 accuracy
at fewer average shots).

Expected outcome. MAKE IT CLEAR: success is a per-query, topology-conditional shot-count map that
(i) significantly improves the sparse/label-mixed topology tail at matched-or-lower average budget,
(ii) does not regress pooled accuracy, (iii) reduces exactly to standard fixed-shot ICL at its
default (E_collapse <= 1e-6), (iv) passes T1-T3, and (v) demonstrably out-performs both a re-tuned
global k and the unstructured AICL-style baselines (B5) on the tail and on the budget-accuracy
frontier -- turning the seed paper's AICL perspective into a structured, safe, tail-validated
method.

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations. F1 (equitable service): adaptive count must not
systematically give harder or under-represented queries (rare classes, dialects, minority
subpopulations) fewer or noisier shots; report per-class and per-subgroup disaggregated accuracy,
and audit that d_x is not a proxy for sensitive attributes, dialect, or register. F2 (do-no-harm
default): the theta = 0 exact disable switch reproduces static-k ICL exactly, is shadow-deployable,
and any active configuration ships only after clearing no-regression on protected slices. F3
(compute/cost equity and transparency): adaptive budget changes per-query latency and token cost,
so cap k_max, report the budget distribution, and log per-query k(d_x) and d_x for auditability.
F4 (scientific integrity): conjectured ranges are marked as such; the E_collapse unit test, the
semi-synthetic topology generators, seeds, and the stratified metric are released; the QPP
covariate is imperfect, so covariate-estimation error is propagated and human spot-checks are
used. F5 (governance and honest scoping): the covariate estimator, clamp band, strata, and pooling
strength are logged and reviewable; consequential deployments (e.g. moderation or clinical-intent
classification) keep a human in the loop; and gains are scoped as improvements over the host's own
static-k, a re-tuned global k, and the unstructured AICL-style baselines -- never as a universal
classification SOTA claim.
